metadata.title: Influenza: Developing Drugs for Treatment and/or Prophylaxis
metadata.note: PDF filename is 18-changes-approved-nda-anda.pdf but document content is the FDA Guidance for Industry: Influenza: Developing Drugs for Treatment and/or Prophylaxis (April 2011, Clinical Antimicrobial). Rules extracted from actual document content.

rules.rule_text: Before initiating clinical trials, sponsors must investigate the mechanism of action and antiviral activity of the candidate drug using multiple types, subtypes, and strains of influenza virus derived from human clinical infections and from animals.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1 – Nonclinical and Early Phase Clinical Development Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Before initiating clinical trials, sponsors should investigate the mechanism of action and antiviral activity of the candidate drug using multiple types, subtypes, and strains of influenza virus derived from human clinical infections and from animals that can serve as sources for new clinical strains.

rules.rule_text: Candidate drugs must be assessed for antiviral activity in cell culture assays and, on the basis of those results, for in vivo activity in appropriate animal models of influenza infection.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1 – Nonclinical and Early Phase Clinical Development Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Candidate drugs should be assessed for antiviral activity in cell culture assays and, on the basis of those assay results, for in vivo activity in appropriate animal models of influenza infection.

rules.rule_text: Virologic assessment and resistance monitoring must be integral to the antiviral drug development program for influenza, and sponsors must address virologic proposals together with nonclinical and clinical trial proposals beginning with pre-IND interactions.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1 – Nonclinical and Early Phase Clinical Development Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Virologic assessment and resistance monitoring are integral to the antiviral drug development for influenza. Sponsors should address virologic proposals together with their proposals for nonclinical studies and clinical trials throughout the development process, beginning with pre-IND interactions with the FDA.

rules.rule_text: Challenge trial proposals must include documentation of the safety testing and biologics IND status (in CBER) of the influenza challenge strains; novel strains of high or unknown pathogenicity must not be used.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1.a – Phase 2A: Challenge trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Proposals for challenge trials should include documentation of the safety testing and biologics investigational new drug application (IND) status (in CBER) of the influenza challenge strains... The use of novel strains of high or unknown pathogenicity is not an option for reasons of ethics, safety, and containment.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide dosing rationale for challenge trials based on animal and human PK and tolerability data, cell culture EC50 values (adjusted for protein binding), and animal model PK/PD data.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1.a – Phase 2A: Challenge trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide dosing rationale for challenge trials on the basis of animal and human PK and tolerability data, cell culture EC50 values (adjusted for protein binding), animal model PK/PD data, and any other relevant information.

rules.rule_text: Challenge trials must not be considered efficacy trials that can support marketing approval, as challenge strains produce milder symptoms than naturally occurring influenza.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1.a – Phase 2A: Challenge trials
rules.verbatim_quote: However, challenge trials should not be considered efficacy trials that can be used to support marketing approval, because challenge strains typically produce a milder set of symptoms compared to naturally occurring influenza, and inoculation in challenge trials may differ from acquisition in naturally occurring infections.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must conduct phase 2 dose-ranging trials before designing phase 3 trials; proceeding directly to phase 3 from phase 1 or phase 2A may fail to produce useful phase 3 data.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.1.b – Phase 2 dose-ranging trials
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that sponsors conduct phase 2 trials before designing phase 3 trials. Proceeding directly to phase 3 from phase 1 or phase 2A trials may fail to produce useful phase 3 data, especially if selection of doses and regimens are not scientifically justified.
rules.exceptions: May proceed if selection of doses and regimens are scientifically justified

rules.rule_text: Treatment and prophylaxis indications for influenza are different indications; each indication must be supported by two adequate and well-controlled trials, unless a single persuasive trial with supportive evidence is sufficient.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.3 – Efficacy Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, treatment and prophylaxis indications for influenza are different indications, and each indication should be supported by two adequate and well-controlled trials. However, sometimes a single persuasive trial may be sufficient for each indication, depending on other supportive evidence.
rules.exceptions: A single persuasive trial may be sufficient with adequate supportive evidence

rules.rule_text: An application for initial approval of a new influenza drug for treatment of uncomplicated influenza must include safety data from at least 1,500 patients at the dose and duration proposed for marketing.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.4 – Safety Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: An application for initial approval of a new influenza drug for the treatment of uncomplicated influenza should include safety data from at least 1,500 patients at the dose and duration proposed for marketing.
rules.exceptions: A larger safety database may be needed if early safety signals have been identified. For drugs for serious influenza in hospitalized patients, approximately 500 patients may suffice.

rules.rule_text: For new drugs showing important clinical benefit in serious influenza in hospitalized patients, an initial safety database of approximately 500 patients may be sufficient for filing a marketing application for treatment of serious influenza.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.4 – Safety Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: For new drugs showing important clinical benefit in serious influenza in hospitalized patients, an initial safety database of approximately 500 patients at the dose and duration proposed for treatment use may be sufficient for filing a marketing application for the treatment of serious influenza.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide a toxicity grading scheme for clinical trials; for prophylaxis or treatment of uncomplicated influenza, toxicity scales appropriate for healthy volunteers must be used.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.4 – Safety Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide a toxicity grading scheme for clinical trials. For trials of influenza prophylaxis or treatment of uncomplicated influenza, we recommend toxicity scales appropriate for use in healthy volunteers.
rules.exceptions: For serious influenza or influenza in patients with underlying medical conditions, other toxicity grading scales can be used

rules.rule_text: For treatment trials of acute uncomplicated influenza, placebo-controlled rather than noninferiority designs must be used because the efficacy of available treatments is modest and variable.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.a – Treatment trials: Acute uncomplicated influenza
rules.verbatim_quote: For trials evaluating treatment of uncomplicated mild to moderate influenza, placebo-controlled rather than noninferiority designs should be used because the risks of receiving placebo are low, and the efficacy of available treatment is modest (1-day difference in time-to-symptom improvement), variable, and cannot be predicted well enough to support an adequate noninferiority margin.
rules.exceptions: Superiority trials with approved antivirals and dose-response trials are also acceptable

rules.rule_text: For seriously ill influenza patients in hospitalized settings, an active-controlled noninferiority trial design is not permissible because no antiviral drug has definitively demonstrated substantial efficacy in serious influenza.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.b – Treatment trials: Serious influenza in hospitalized patients
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, because no trial has definitively demonstrated substantial clinical efficacy of an antiviral drug in serious influenza or in hospitalized patients, determination of a noninferiority margin is not possible. As a result, an active-controlled noninferiority trial is not possible.

rules.rule_text: For prophylaxis trials, in settings where there are definite recommendations from public health entities for drug prophylaxis (e.g., after onset of an outbreak in a nursing home), placebo controls will not be possible.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.c – Prophylaxis trials
rules.verbatim_quote: In settings in which there are definite recommendations from public health entities for drug prophylaxis (e.g., after onset of an outbreak within a nursing home), placebo controls will not be possible.

rules.rule_text: To fulfill Pediatric Research Equity Act requirements and extend treatment and/or prophylaxis indications to pediatric age groups, sponsors must conduct adequate and well-controlled trials with clinical efficacy endpoints and complete safety evaluations; PK and safety trials alone are insufficient.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.2 – Trial Population
rules.verbatim_quote: To fulfill the Pediatric Research Equity Act requirements and extend treatment and/or prophylaxis indications to pediatric age groups, sponsors need to conduct adequate and well-controlled trials with clinical efficacy endpoints and complete safety evaluations. PK and safety trials will not be considered adequate to extend the indications to children, particularly those younger than 12 years of age.

rules.rule_text: Individuals who have recently received a live-virus influenza vaccine generally must be excluded from clinical trial participation, as antiviral drugs may have deleterious effects on response to a live-virus vaccine.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3 – Entry Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Antiviral drugs might theoretically have deleterious effects on response to a live-virus influenza vaccine if they are administered in the same time period as the vaccine and inhibit replication of the vaccine virus. Therefore, individuals who have recently received a live-virus influenza vaccine generally should be excluded from participation.
rules.exceptions: Drug effects in response to inactivated vaccine are less likely

rules.rule_text: Double-blinding of treatments is required in influenza clinical trials given the subjectivity of endpoints and the potential for confusion between natural variability of influenza and drug effects.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 – Blinding
rules.verbatim_quote: Double-blinding of treatments is important, given the subjectivity of endpoints and the potential for confusion between the natural variability of influenza and the drug effects (either beneficial or adverse).

rules.rule_text: Virologic endpoints must not be used as primary endpoints in phase 3 treatment trials, as there is no established predictive relationship between magnitude/timing of viral reductions and extent of clinical benefit.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.a – Efficacy Endpoints: General considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Currently, we do not consider virologic endpoints to be appropriate primary endpoints in phase 3 treatment trials because: (1) there is no established predictive relationship between magnitude and timing of viral reductions and extent of clinical benefit of how a patient 'feels, functions, or survives'; (2) optimal sampling site, methods including collection procedures, and assays for clinically relevant virologic measurements have not been established; and (3) available data suggest substantial variability in results and conclusions using different methods for collecting and analyzing virologic data.
rules.exceptions: Virologic measurements are important secondary endpoints and can be used as components of entry criteria

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint in treatment trials for acute uncomplicated influenza in adults must be time to a pre-defined level of symptom improvement, incorporating fever with a constellation of symptoms.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.b – Treatment of acute uncomplicated illness
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary endpoint in treatment trials in adults for acute uncomplicated influenza should be the time to a pre-defined level of symptom improvement. Components of the primary endpoint should include fever with a constellation of symptoms (e.g., cough, coryza, headache, body aches, sore throat).

rules.rule_text: The primary analysis population for both primary and additional analyses must include all subjects with confirmed influenza (intent-to-treat infected population).
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.b – Treatment of acute uncomplicated illness
rules.verbatim_quote: The population for both the primary analysis and additional analyses should include all subjects with confirmed influenza (i.e., intent-to-treat (ITT) infected population).
rules.exceptions: Exploratory analyses of on-treatment or per-protocol populations may be used to identify dosing problems

rules.rule_text: For seriously ill influenza patients requiring hospitalization, clinical trial proposals must be provided for advance discussion and the primary endpoint must include clinical signs/symptoms, duration of hospitalization, time to normalization of vital signs and oxygenation, and mortality.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.c – Treatment of seriously ill hospitalized patients
rules.verbatim_quote: For seriously ill influenza patients requiring hospitalization, a primary endpoint should include clinical signs and symptoms, duration of hospitalization, time to normalization of vital signs and oxygenation, requirements for supplemental oxygen or assisted ventilation, and mortality... clinical trial proposals should be provided for advance discussion.
rules.exceptions: Choice of endpoint may depend on clinical setting and/or viral strains

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint for prophylaxis trials must be the occurrence of symptomatic, laboratory-confirmed influenza.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.d – Prophylaxis
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary endpoint for prophylaxis trials should be the occurrence of symptomatic, laboratory-confirmed influenza.

rules.rule_text: If sponsors propose claims of reduction in complications, objective criteria must be delineated and justified prospectively, and methods of diagnosis and confirmation of secondary infections must be described in detail in the protocol.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.6.e – Reduction in complications
rules.verbatim_quote: If sponsors propose claims of reduction in complications, objective criteria should be delineated and justified prospectively. Information on the specifics of diagnosis and management should be collected in the protocol... Diagnoses of secondary complications should be confirmed by objective clinical, laboratory, and medical imaging findings as appropriate. In the absence of such objective confirmation, labeling statements regarding efficacy claims for the prevention of secondary complications are unlikely.

rules.rule_text: Protocols must include frequent self-assessments continuing until resolution of all clinical signs and symptoms, along with observer assessments at less frequent intervals.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.7 – Trial Procedures and Timing of Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Protocols should include frequent self-assessments (e.g., use of diary cards), along with observer assessments at less frequent intervals or as triggered by self-assessment results. Self-assessments should continue until resolution of all clinical signs and symptoms.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide a protocol with a statistical analysis plan for FDA review and concurrence before subject enrollment.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.8 – Statistical Considerations for Phase 3 Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide a protocol with a statistical analysis plan for review for FDA concurrence before subject enrollment.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must avoid censoring subjects in the ITT infected population in short-term influenza treatment trials and must have an explicit and adequate plan to address missing data.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.8.a – Statistical Considerations: Treatment trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should avoid censoring subjects in the ITT infected population in these short-term trials. There should be an explicit and adequate plan to address issues relating to missing data.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must retain pertinent investigational records such as diary cards and copies of original laboratory sheets so that they are available at the time of any FDA inspection.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.8.b – Statistical Considerations: Prophylaxis trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors must ensure that pertinent investigational records such as diary cards and copies of the original laboratory sheets are retained so that they are available at the time of any FDA inspection (21 CFR 312.62(c)).

rules.rule_text: Accelerated approval (Subpart H) using surrogate endpoints is not applicable to influenza drug development because clinical benefits are assessed over a short time period and no surrogate marker has been reliably identified as reasonably likely to predict important clinical outcomes.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.9 – Accelerated Approval (Subpart H) Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: The criteria described in regulations in 21 CFR part 314, subpart H (accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint considered reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit in patients with a serious or life-threatening disease) have not been used for approval of influenza antivirals, and are unlikely to be appropriate in most instances, because typical influenza clinical trials involve direct assessment of immediate clinical outcomes for an acute uncomplicated illness.
rules.exceptions: Proposals for accelerated approval may be submitted for FDA discussion

rules.rule_text: The animal rule (21 CFR 314 Subpart I) is not an appropriate mechanism for approval of influenza drugs; treatment trials in virus-challenged animals are not a substitute for clinical trials.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.4 – Animal Rule (Subpart I) and Animal Model Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: However, because human clinical trials in influenza are feasible, ethical, and the best approach for characterizing safety and efficacy, the animal rule (21 CFR 314, subpart I, and corresponding biologics regulations in 21 CFR 601, subpart H) is not an appropriate mechanism for approval of influenza drugs... Thus, treatment trials in virus-challenged animals are not a substitute for clinical trials.
rules.exceptions: Animal data can support IND access or EUA mechanisms

rules.rule_text: For combination antiviral therapy, trial designs must demonstrate the activity of each component of the combination therapy (i.e., contribution of each component to the combination) generally using factorial designs.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.3 – Efficacy Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, trial designs should demonstrate the activity of each component of combination therapy (i.e., contribution of each component to the combination). Establishing the contribution of each component, generally using factorial designs, is important whether the proposed combination contains two or more antiviral drugs (e.g., a co-packaged combination, or a fixed-dose combination) or a drug and a therapeutic biological product.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response modeling of phase 2 and/or phase 3 data must be included in a new drug application (NDA) to characterize relationships between drug concentrations and efficacy and safety.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.2.c – Modeling considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response modeling of phase 2 and/or phase 3 data should be included in a new drug application (NDA) to characterize relationships between drug concentrations and efficacy and safety.

rules.rule_text: For drugs indicated for prophylaxis of influenza, carcinogenicity studies in rats and mice must be carried out before approval because such drugs are used in an intermittent manner over multiple influenza seasons.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.1 – Relevant Nonclinical Safety Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: If, on the other hand, the drug is indicated for the prophylaxis of influenza, carcinogenicity studies in rats and mice should be carried out before approval because drugs used frequently in an intermittent manner in the treatment of chronic or recurrent conditions generally should be supported by such studies.
rules.exceptions: Drugs indicated only for treatment of influenza do not generally require carcinogenicity studies

rules.rule_text: Baseline susceptibility and emergence of resistance to the trial drug must be examined in clinical trials regardless of trial design; samples must be retained for future resistance testing until trial completion and data analysis if standardized susceptibility testing methods are not available.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.7 – Trial Procedures and Timing of Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Baseline susceptibility and emergence of resistance to the trial drug should be examined in clinical trials, regardless of trial designs. If standardized and generally accepted susceptibility testing methods are not available, samples should be retained for future testing until the trial has been completed and data have been analyzed.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide detailed viral resistance monitoring plans that describe proposed analyses, sample collection timing, assay characteristics with different influenza types and subtypes, and assay methodologies; these plans must be provided to the FDA early in development.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.7 – Trial Procedures and Timing of Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide detailed viral resistance monitoring plans that describe proposed analyses, sample collection timing, assay characteristics with different influenza types and subtypes, and assay methodologies. Such plans should be provided to the FDA for review early in development, and updates to the plans should be discussed at appropriate intervals during development.

rules.rule_text: When sponsors rely on foreign clinical trial data to support marketing approval, they must supplement the foreign data with information about circulating influenza strains, patterns of clinical illness, trial population demographics, standards of medical care, and applicability to the US context.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.2 – Drug Development Population
rules.verbatim_quote: When sponsors rely on foreign clinical trial data — whether as part of multinational trials including the United States or as part of trials conducted entirely outside the United States — to support the marketing approval of their candidate drugs, they should supplement the foreign data with information about circulating influenza strains, patterns of clinical illness, trial population demographics, standards of medical care, and the use of other medical interventions in the countries where the trials were conducted.
